title: podman machine start/stop without args does not reflect actual default VM
body:
### Issue Description

When I flag my custom VM as a default VM (`podman system connection default <my_vm>`) and then use `podman machine start` or `stop` without arguments it does not try to start/stop <my_vm> but the original `podman-machine-default`.

If this is not a bug but feature, please make sure the [documentation](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-machine-start.1.html) is more specific.

```shell
> podman version
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.4.2
API Version:  4.4.2
Go Version:   go1.20.1
Git Commit:   74afe26887f814d1c39925a1624851ef3590e79c
Built:        Thu Feb 23 16:59:21 2023
OS/Arch:      darwin/arm64

Server:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.4.2
API Version:  4.4.2
Go Version:   go1.19.6
Built:        Wed Mar  1 12:22:39 2023
OS/Arch:      linux/arm64
```

(see below for `podman info`)

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue (for `stop`)
1. Verify which VM is the default one using `podman system connection list`
2. `podman machine stop` should stop the default VM

Steps to reproduce the issue (for `start`)
1. Create a custom <my_vm> VM next to existing `podman-machine-default`
3. `podman system connection default <my_vm>`
4. Verify which VM is the default one using `podman system connection list`
5. Make sure all machine VMs are stopped
6. `podman machine start`


### Describe the results you received

### For the `stop` command:

1. My custom VM is running
```shell
% podman machine list
NAME                    VM TYPE     CREATED       LAST UP            CPUS        MEMORY      DISK SIZE
testOpenSearch*         qemu        25 hours ago  Currently running  4           34.36GB     107.4GB
podman-machine-default  qemu        25 hours ago  42 minutes ago     1           2.147GB     107.4GB
```
2. Stop the default VM (it just tells me that the `podman-machine-default` is stipped, but that is not the default VM)
```shell
 % podman machine stop
Machine "podman-machine-default" stopped successfully
```

### For the `start` command:

1. I have two VMs, one of them is custom one and set as the default
```shell
% podman machine list               
NAME                    VM TYPE     CREATED       LAST UP         CPUS        MEMORY      DISK SIZE
testOpenSearch*         qemu        25 hours ago  23 seconds ago  4           34.36GB     107.4GB
podman-machine-default  qemu        25 hours ago  45 minutes ago  1           2.147GB     107.4GB

% podman system connection list  
Name                         URI                                                         Identity                                        Default
podman-machine-default       ssh://core@localhost:60582/run/user/501/podman/podman.sock  /Users/lukas.vlcek/.ssh/podman-machine-default  false
podman-machine-default-root  ssh://root@localhost:60582/run/podman/podman.sock           /Users/lukas.vlcek/.ssh/podman-machine-default  false
testOpenSearch               ssh://core@localhost:60707/run/user/501/podman/podman.sock  /Users/lukas.vlcek/.ssh/testOpenSearch          true
testOpenSearch-root          ssh://root@localhost:60707/run/podman/podman.sock           /Users/lukas.vlcek/.ssh/testOpenSearch          false
```
2. Start the default VM
```shell
% podman machine start
Starting machine "podman-machine-default"
Waiting for VM ...
Mounting volume... /Users:/Users
Mounting volume... /private:/private
Mounting volume... /var/folders:/var/folders

This machine is currently configured in rootless mode. If your containers
require root permissions (e.g. ports < 1024), or if you run into compatibility
issues with non-podman clients, you can switch using the following command: 

	podman machine set --rootful

API forwarding listening on: /var/run/docker.sock
Docker API clients default to this address. You do not need to set DOCKER_HOST.

Machine "podman-machine-default" started successfully
```
Why does it not start the default VM?

### Describe the results you expected

If `podman machine start/stop` is not given any arguments then it should start the default VM as defined by `podman machine list` or `podman system connection list`. Otherwise it is confusing to me and the [documentation](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-machine-start.1.html) does not provide any hint about why this is the case.

### podman info output


### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

Running podman `4.4.2` since it is the latest that `brew` offers at the moment (I do not feel like messing with `brew` much at this point of my research). I know it is not the most recent release (as of writing) but according to release notes none of `4.4.3` or `4.4.4` brings any fixes in this area. At least the source code of the command looks unchanged: https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/cmd/podman/machine/start.go#L51

### Additional information

Additional information like issue happens only occasionally or issue happens with a particular architecture or on a particular setting

comments.body:
This is expected, podman system connection manages only connections not machine VMs.
I am not aware of any options to change the default machine name. You have to use the machine commands with the VM name as argument.

That said I think your request makes sense as feature request, especially when there is only a single VM I see little reason to require the name to be specified on the cli every time.  

cc @baude @ashley-cui
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

This is the intended behavior, as the default connection is different than the default machine. I can update the docs to reflect this. 

The idea of being able configure the default machine name is interesting.

@ashley-cui documenting the current behavior sounds like a good idea to me.

I personally don't like to default to some machine when no argument is given. Podman expects arguments for most commands (e.g., container, pod, volume, etc.).

> I personally don't like to default to some machine when no argument is given. Podman expects arguments for most commands (e.g., container, pod, volume, etc.).

I agree, it also makes argument parsing much more complicated then it should be but it is what it is, so we have to live with this design decision now.
